target: data/sections.json part4 §4.2.7 prose (H3 fix — ISS-1608 lateral-velocity propagation) + docs/assets/eclipse-geometry-comparison.svg annotation panel (H2 cleanup — ISS-1607 stationary-spot framing)
status_summary: Both holes are partially addressed in the integrated §4.2.7 already (the prose says 'narrow ~1.3-km-wide track' instead of 'stationary spot', the SVG annotation panel was patched with an extra line). However the H3 parenthetical is mathematically wrong (claims slower velocity 'leaves the 1.3-km totality-circle diameter unchanged' — true of the diameter but misleading because totality DURATION at one observer scales as 1.3/v and DOES change), and the H2 SVG panel patch leaves a redundant red-text line that duplicates the first bullet. This expansion provides the corrected prose and the cleaned-up SVG annotation panel.
holes_addressed: H2 (ISS-1607): SVG annotation panel still shows '1.3 km totality circle' (static framing) plus an awkward red-text patch line '~1.3 km track (≈ 1/220 of observed path)' that duplicates the first bullet. Defender's 'the spot moves' rebuttal not explicitly closed in the figure.

H3 (ISS-1608): prose parenthetical is mathematically wrong about what 25 km/min changes. It correctly says the totality-circle DIAMETER is unchanged but implies that the per-observer totality DURATION is also unchanged. Per the formula totality_sec = (1.3 km / v km·min⁻¹) · 60, totality at one observer = 0.9 sec at v=87 and ~3 sec at v=25 — same number that EXP-254 math_verification step 6 acknowledged but never propagated into prose. ISS-1608 also asks for an angular-velocity translation so the dome author cannot claim a unit-mismatch dismissal.

patch_specs.uniqueness_check: old_string appears exactly once in part4.html (verified). The phrase 'As the Moon transits at ≈ 87 km/min' is unique to §4.2.7.
patch_specs.old_string: As the Moon transits at ≈ 87 km/min (a 24-hour circuit of a 20,015-km disc; this is an upper bound assuming the Moon orbits the disc rim — a more central orbit yields ~25 km/min, which lengthens penumbra crossing to a few minutes but leaves the 1.3-km totality-circle diameter unchanged), a single observer sitting under the totality path experiences totality for 1.3 km / 87 km·min−1 · 60 ≈ 0.9 seconds; a station within the penumbra band sees a partial eclipse for at most 60 seconds.
patch_specs.new_string: As the Moon transits at ≈ 87 km/min (a 24-hour circuit of the 20,015-km disc rim — an upper bound for a rim-radius orbit, equivalent to ~2°/min apparent shadow speed at zenith; a more typical mid-disc orbit corresponding to the mid-northern latitudes where the actual August 2026 path lies yields ~25 km/min, or ~0.5°/min at hMoon= 2,534 km), a single observer sitting under the totality path experiences totality for 1.3 km / 87 km·min−1 · 60 ≈ 0.9 seconds at the rim-orbit upper bound, lengthening to about 3 seconds at 25 km/min. The argument is robust either way: 3 seconds vs. the observed 138-second maximum is still a ~45× shortfall. A station within the penumbra band sees a partial eclipse for at most 60 seconds at the upper bound, or a few minutes at the slower mid-disc speed.
patch_specs.rationale: Replace the broken parenthetical (which contains a math error) with one that correctly propagates 25 km/min into single-observer totality duration (~3 sec) AND adds the angular-velocity cross-check (~0.5°/min and ~2°/min at h=2,534 km) as ISS-1608 requested. Argument-robustness sentence ('45× shortfall') stays decisive. Word count grows from 95 to 165 — adds two short factual sentences.

patch_specs.uniqueness_check: 5-line block starting with 'x="68" y="160">• 1.3 km totality circle' is unique to this SVG file.
patch_specs.old_string:
<text class="eg-num" x="68" y="160">• 1.3 km totality circle</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="68" y="178">  (≈ 1/220 of observed)</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="68" y="200">• 0.9 sec at one observer</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="68" y="218">  (≈ 1/150 of observed 138 s)</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="68" y="236" fill="var(--semantic-crit,#7a2e2e)">~1.3 km track (≈ 1/220 of observed path)</text>
patch_specs.new_string:
<text class="eg-num" x="68" y="160">• 1.3 km totality track</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="68" y="178">  (≈ 1/220 of observed 294 km)</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="68" y="200">• 0.9 sec at one observer</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="68" y="218">  (≈ 1/150 of observed 138 s)</text>
  <text class="eg-num" x="68" y="236" fill="var(--semantic-crit,#7a2e2e)">Track moves; per-observer ~0.9 sec</text>
patch_specs.rationale: Three coordinated edits: (a) first bullet 'totality circle' → 'totality track' addresses the static-framing rebuttal that ISS-1607 names. (b) First-bullet ratio gains '294 km' to make the comparison reference explicit. (c) Redundant red patch line is replaced with 'Track moves; per-observer ~0.9 sec' — preserves the visual emphasis of the highlighted line while explicitly closing the 'the spot moves with the Sun' rebuttal: yes, the track moves, but the per-observer dwell is still ~0.9 sec. Y-coordinates and font classes preserved.

math_verification.rim_orbit_velocity: 2π·20,015 km / 1440 min = 87.33 km/min ✓ (matches existing prose; EXP-254 curmudgeon also reproduced this independently)
math_verification.mid_disc_velocity: At a flat-earth-Mercator latitude ~64°N (radius 5,730 km), 2π·5,730 / 1440 = 25.0 km/min ✓. The actual Aug 2026 path centerline averages ~70°N (radius 4,448 km, v=19.4 km/min) — even slower than 25 km/min, so 25 km/min is a charitable middle-ground charge.
math_verification.totality_at_one_observer: totality_sec = 1.3 km / v(km·min⁻¹) · 60. At v=87 → 0.90 sec ✓; at v=25 → 3.12 sec; at v=19.4 → 4.02 sec. The patch uses ~3 sec rounded.
math_verification.standard_moon_apparent_angular: Moon apparent angular motion across the sky from a fixed Earth observer ≈ 0.25°/min (dominated by Earth's rotation 360°/24h). The dome moon at zenith moves through a fixed observer's sky at 1.97°/min — about 8× faster than the actual Moon. This is the comparable-units cross-check ISS-1608 asked for.
math_verification.robustness_factor: 45× shortfall claim: 138.2 sec / 3.12 sec = 44.3× ✓ (rounded to 45×). At the slower 19.4 km/min the shortfall drops to 138.2/4.02 = 34.4×, still decisive. The patch states ~45× to be conservative on the dome's behalf.

kernel_of_truth: The dome's lateral velocity figure is in the right neighborhood of the actual eclipse-shadow ground speed (NASA reports ~30–50 km/min for the August 2026 path central segment). The 87 km/min rim-orbit upper bound is faster; the 25 km/min mid-disc figure is similar to the standard physics ground speed. So the dome's velocity assumption is not patently absurd in linear-units terms — the dome author can defensibly claim 'the moon transits at speeds comparable to the actual shadow speed.' The kernel here is real. What the kernel does NOT save is the ratio of totality DURATION to PATH WIDTH: even with a velocity in the right ballpark, a 1.3-km-wide track produces 0.9–3 seconds of totality at any single observer vs. the observed 138-second maximum. The geometric mismatch is 45× even at the most charitable velocity.

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome moon orbits at angular velocity ~0.5°/min (the dome author writes in degrees per minute, not km per minute), so the linear-speed figures are unit-mismatched against his model.
anticipated_objections.response: The corrected parenthetical now provides BOTH unit systems: 87 km/min ≈ 2°/min at zenith, and 25 km/min ≈ 0.5°/min. The 0.5°/min figure matches the slower mid-disc orbit charity. Either way, totality at one observer is 0.9–3 seconds vs. the observed 138 seconds — the ratio argument is invariant under unit choice.

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome predicts a moving spot (the Sun moves, the Moon moves, the shadow moves with them), not a stationary spot. Calling the dome geometry 'a track' rather than 'a path' is splitting hairs.
anticipated_objections.response: The patch agrees: the shadow does move (a track, not a stationary spot). The SVG and prose both say 'track' now. The substantive critique is that the track is 1.3 km wide and per-observer dwell time is 0.9–3 sec — moving slowly across a wide path is not what the eclipse data shows; the data shows a 294-km-wide path with up to 138-second totality at a fixed observer. Calling it a 'track' rather than a 'spot' costs nothing and removes a rhetorical opening.

anticipated_objections.objection: The 25 km/min figure assumes a flat-earth-Mercator latitude projection (latitude → disc-radius linear in (90−lat)/90). Other dome-coordinate models exist; the 25 km/min figure may not be portable.
anticipated_objections.response: Acknowledged. The patch says 'mid-northern latitudes where the actual August 2026 path lies' rather than committing to a specific projection. The robustness claim ('argument is robust either way') is what carries the weight: 0.9 sec to ~4 sec at the slowest plausible orbit, vs. 138 sec observed. No flat-earth coordinate system rescues the dome from a 30–150× duration shortfall on a 1.3-km-wide track.

anticipated_objections.objection: By giving the dome a charitable 25 km/min figure, the patch weakens the 'observed/dome ratio = 1/150' headline number used elsewhere in the section. We've conceded ground.
anticipated_objections.response: We've made the section's argument MORE robust at the cost of one specific headline ratio. The 1/150 ratio is preserved as 'at the rim-orbit upper bound' and the new 1/45 ratio is added as 'at the mid-disc speed.' A defender can no longer cherry-pick an even slower velocity to escape — both extremes have been shown to fail. This is rhetorical strengthening, not concession.

cross_section_impact.p4_421_cross_reference: §4.2.1 references the §4.2.7 totality figures briefly. Quick grep for '0.9 sec' or '0.66 km' or '1/150' in other §4.2 sections shows no in-line numerical duplication — only the existing summary at idx ~297 in part4.html, which says 'umbra ground radius of 0.66 km and a penumbra ground radius of R_p ≈ 42 km' (still correct).
cross_section_impact.no_other_sections_affected: I checked data/sections.json for '87 km/min', '25 km/min', 'stationary spot', 'totality circle' — only the §4.2.7 block uses these phrasings. data/wins.json and data/predictions.json do not reference these specific numbers (verified by grep).
cross_section_impact.docs_index_html: docs/index.html will be regenerated by node build.js html on next publish — auto-resolves any literal duplicates.

decider_instructions: 1. Apply P1 (substring_replace in data/sections.json part4.html). Confirm uniqueness via grep before substitution.
3. Run tests/run.sh — should still pass; no test references these specific phrasings.

4. Run node build.js html — regenerates docs/index.html with the corrected prose. The eclipse-geometry-comparison.svg is referenced by in §4.2.7 and will be served by Pages with the cleaned-up annotations.

5. Close ISS-1607 with fix_details: 'SVG annotation panel cleaned: 1.3 km totality circle → totality track; redundant patch line replaced with Track moves; per-observer ~0.9 sec, explicitly closing the the-spot-moves-with-the-Sun rebuttal.'

6. Close ISS-1608 with fix_details: 'Prose parenthetical corrected: previously claimed slower velocity left totality-circle diameter unchanged (true but misleading — duration scales as 1.3/v); now correctly propagates ~3 sec at 25 km/min and adds angular-velocity cross-check (~2°/min at zenith for rim orbit, ~0.5°/min for mid-disc orbit) per ISS-1608 unit-system request.'
7. Update tracker entry for EXP-272: status=integrated, integrated_at=<now>, integrated_by=decider (per HNOTE-OPERATOR-DECIDER-EXPANSION-TRACKER-SYNC-001 direction A).

exp_meta.rationale: EXP-254 was integrated as a section-new addition. The curmudgeon's c1 review identified six holes; H1, H4, H5, H6 were already cleaned up before/during integration. H2 (stationary-spot framing) and H3 (lateral-velocity charity) were partially patched but with errors that surfaced post-integration. EXP-272 finishes the c1 follow-up cleanly.
